chapters.title: The Classroom Tic
chapters.intro: Alex is sitting in math class when his shoulder suddenly jerks and he makes a short throat-clearing sound. The kids around him giggle. The teacher looks up.
chapters.question: What do you do?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Laugh along with the others — it is pretty funny
choices.outcome: Alex sees you laughing. His shoulders sag, and his tic gets worse. He feels alone in a room full of classmates.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Whisper 'stop it' to Alex so the teacher doesn't get angry
choices.outcome: Alex hears you and tries hard to hold the tic in. But trying to stop a tic only makes it stronger — and now he feels embarrassed too.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ignore it and keep working on your worksheet
choices.outcome: You don't add to the attention. Alex appreciates that you didn't react. It's a quiet, kind choice.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Keep working and give Alex a small encouraging nod
choices.outcome: Your nod tells Alex: 'I see you, and it's okay.' He relaxes a little, and the tics calm down. You just made his day easier.

facts: Tourette Syndrome is a neurological condition — Alex cannot control his tics any more than you can control a sneeze.
Stress and being watched can make tics worse, not better.
chapters.reflection: Have you ever tried to stop yourself from sneezing? How did that feel?
chapters.myth: He's clearing his throat on purpose to disrupt class
chapters.reality: Tics are involuntary — like trying to stop a sneeze mid-sneeze

chapters.title: Lunch Table
chapters.intro: It's lunch. Alex sits down at your table. Mid-bite, his arm jerks and he knocks over his juice. Some kids move away. He looks embarrassed.
chapters.question: What do you do?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Move away too — you don't want juice on your clothes
choices.outcome: Alex sees the whole table empty around him. He picks up his tray and eats alone in the corner.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Stare at him to see if it happens again
choices.outcome: Alex feels your eyes on him. The pressure makes his tics flare up even more. He stops eating and looks at the floor.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hand him some napkins without making a big deal
choices.outcome: You quietly slide some napkins his way. Alex cleans up, takes a breath, and keeps eating. No drama. Just friendship.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Say loudly 'It's okay everyone, he just has a condition'
choices.outcome: You meant well, but now everyone is staring. Alex turns red and wishes he could disappear.

facts: Drawing attention to someone's tic — even kindly — can make them feel more self-conscious and increase tic frequency.
chapters.reflection: When something embarrassing happens to you, do you want everyone to notice, or do you want it to pass quietly?
chapters.myth: He's clumsy and careless at the table
chapters.reality: Motor tics can strike at any moment — attention from others makes them worse, not better

chapters.title: Group Project
chapters.intro: Your teacher assigns a group project. Alex is in your group. A classmate whispers 'Can we ask to switch? He'll mess up the presentation with his tics.'
chapters.question: What do you do?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Agree — it's easier without the complications
choices.outcome: Alex overhears. He spends the rest of the week feeling like a problem instead of a teammate.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Say nothing and hope the teacher changes it
choices.outcome: Saying nothing is its own kind of answer. The classmate assumes you agree, and Alex notices the cold silence.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Tell the classmate quietly that's not fair
choices.outcome: You stand up for Alex without making a scene. The group works together. Alex's part of the presentation is great.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ask Alex directly if he wants to work alone
choices.outcome: You meant to be kind, but it sounds like you're pushing him out. Alex says 'sure' — but he wanted to be included.

facts: Students with Tourette's are often academically capable and want to participate fully. Social exclusion is more disabling than the tics themselves.
chapters.reflection: What's the difference between including someone and just tolerating them?
chapters.myth: His tics will ruin the group's work
chapters.reality: Students with Tourette's are just as capable — exclusion hurts far more than tics ever could
